message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  COVID-19, caused by SARS-CoV-2 infection, has recently been announced as a pandemic all over the world. Plenty of diagnostic, preventive and therapeutic knowledges have been enriched from clinical studies since December 2019. However, animal models, particularly non-human primate models, are urgently needed for critical questions that could not be answered in clinical patients, evaluations of anti-viral drugs and vaccines. In this study, two families of non-human primates, Old world monkeys (12
                  <jats:italic>Macaca mulatta</jats:italic>
                  , 6
                  <jats:italic>Macaca fascicularis</jats:italic>
                  ) and New world monkeys (6
                  <jats:italic>Callithrix jacchus</jats:italic>
                  ), were experimentally inoculated with SARS-CoV-2. Clinical signs were recorded. Samples were collected for analysis of viral shedding, viremia and histopathological examination. Increased body temperature was observed in 100% (12/12)
                  <jats:italic>M. mulatta</jats:italic>
                  , 33.3% (2/6)
                  <jats:italic>M. fascicularis</jats:italic>
                  and none (0/6) of
                  <jats:italic>C. jacchus</jats:italic>
                  post inoculation of SARS-CoV-2. All of
                  <jats:italic>M. mulatta</jats:italic>
                  and
                  <jats:italic>M. fascicularis</jats:italic>
                  showed chest radiographic abnormality. Viral genomes were detected in nasal swabs, throat swabs, anal swabs and blood from all 3 species of monkeys. Viral shedding from upper respiratory samples reached the peak between day 6 and day 8 post inoculation. From necropsied
                  <jats:italic>M. mulatta</jats:italic>
                  and
                  <jats:italic>M. fascicularis</jats:italic>
                  , the tissues showing virus positive were mainly lung, weasand, bronchus and spleen. No viral genome was seen in any of tissues from 2 necropsied
                  <jats:italic>C. jacchus.</jats:italic>
                  Severe gross lesions and histopathological changes were observed in lung, heart and stomach of SARS-CoV-2 infected animals. In summary, we have established a NHP model for COVID-19, which could be used to evaluate drugs and vaccines, and investigate viral pathogenesis.
                  <jats:italic>M. mulatta</jats:italic>
                  is the most susceptible to SARS-CoV-2 infection, followed by
                  <jats:italic>M. fascicularis</jats:italic>
                  and
                  <jats:italic>C. jacchus.</jats:italic>
                </jats:p>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>One Sentence Summary</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    <jats:italic>M. mulatta</jats:italic>
                    is the most susceptible to SARS-CoV-2 infection as compared to
                    <jats:italic>M. fascicularis</jats:italic>
                    and
                    <jats:italic>C. jacchus</jats:italic>
                    .
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Comparison of SARS-CoV-2 infections among 3 species of non-human primates
